q: Will I actually see the aurora?
a: We plan expeditions around solar activity and keep reserve nights, and more than nine in ten of our northern groups see the aurora. But the sky is the sky — we no longer make guarantees, and we would rather earn your trust than sell you certainty.

q: How fit do I need to be?
a: Most expeditions are gentle: short transfers and level walks. High-altitude sites such as Atacama and Mauna Kea require acclimatisation, which we build into the itinerary and explain in advance.

q: I have no telescope or camera.
a: Nor do you need one. We provide telescopes, binoculars and tripods. For photography you may hire a camera or simply use your phone — the guide will help you keep a frame either way.
Never more than twelve travellers to a guide. It is a deliberate limit: enough time at the eyepiece for everyone, and enough quiet for the sky to do its work.
Children from eight are welcome. Some nights are long and cold, so for younger travellers we tend to suggest the gentler latitudes — we are glad to advise.
Free cancellation up to thirty days before departure, with a full refund. After that a deposit is held. We also offer weather insurance for the anxious.
Every expedition is carbon-compensated through verified projects, and we work with local lodges and guides wherever we go. The dark sky is a resource we are trying not to spend.
